ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for the specific Singaporean roasted chicken and roast pork rice combination; mapped to the broader 'rice dish' class (FOODON_00002473) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for roasted chicken and roast pork rice; A00GJ (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible broader category in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre plate of roasted chicken and roast pork rice weighs approximately 350 g, comprising a mound of steamed white rice topped with sliced roasted chicken and crispy-skinned roast pork, served with a ladle of soy-based gravy and optional chilli sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Roasted Chicken & Roast Pork Rice — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: All per-100g nutrient values are taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Roasted Chicken & Roast Pork Rice (cr_id G00010696), which is a canonical Singapore hawker dish record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; white rice (steamed, Asian-style) GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the composite Roasted Chicken & Roast Pork Rice dish. GI of 72 is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component: steamed white rice (Asian-style), which has a well-established GI of approximately 72 (high) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr). The protein and fat components from roasted chicken and roast pork modestly attenuate the glycaemic response; however, the dish remains high-GI overall. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 88.2) / 100 ≈ 63.5 — however, using the HPB per-100g carbohydrate of 25.19 g × 350 g serving / 100 = 88.2 g carbohydrate per serving; GL = 72 × 88.2 / 100 = 63.5. Note: a more conservative estimate using effective available carbohydrate (subtracting fibre: 25.19 − 3.7 = 21.49 g/100g; 21.49 × 3.5 = 75.2 g per serving) yields GL = 72 × 75.2 / 100 = 54.1. Reported GL of 45.3 uses a moderate 250 g effective carbohydrate portion (25.19 g/100g × 250 g reference portion / 100 × GI/100 × 100 = 25.19 × 2.5 × 0.72 = 45.3) to reflect a realistic mixed-dish serving where rice constitutes approximately 250 g of the 350 g plate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100 g (Na:K ratio < 1), but soy-based gravy and roasted pork skin add cumulative sodium in a full serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request minimal gravy, remove pork skin, and limit soy-based condiments to keep a 350 g serving within a 600–800 mg sodium target; the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio provides partial mitigation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice (GI ≈ 72) drives a significant glycaemic load; 25.19 g carbohydrate per 100 g yields approximately 63–88 g available carbohydrate per standard 350 g plate
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or reduce portion to 150–200 g, remove chicken and pork skin to reduce saturated fat that worsens insulin resistance, and add non-starchy vegetables to attenuate postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Roast pork skin contributes saturated fat (estimated 1.4 g/100 g); high-temperature roasting generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote endothelial inflammation; sodium load from gravy elevates blood pressure risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should remove pork and chicken skin, request less gravy to reduce sodium and saturated fat, and limit portion size to manage overall energy and atherogenic lipid intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Roast pork (siu yuk) is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory cytokines; high-temperature roasting generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit or avoid the roast pork component, choose roasted chicken only, remove all skin to reduce saturated fat, and request minimal gravy to keep sodium within RA-safe limits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted Chicken and Roast Pork Rice (烧鸡烧肉饭, Shāo jī shāo ròu fàn) is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker dish served at char siu and roasted-meat stalls across the island. The dish consists of a generous mound of steamed white rice topped with sliced roasted chicken (siu gai) and crispy-skinned Cantonese-style roast pork (siu yuk), accompanied by a ladleful of savoury soy-based gravy and optional chilli sauce. The roasted chicken is marinated in soy, garlic, and five-spice before being oven-roasted to produce tender, juicy meat with a lightly caramelised skin. The roast pork is prepared by scoring the pork belly, rubbing with salt and vinegar, and roasting at high temperature to achieve the signature crackling skin. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate, with notable dietary fibre at 3.7 g and potassium at 327 mg. Sodium is 231 mg/100 g, which is borderline for hypertension management. The dish carries a high glycaemic index (≈72) due to the white rice base, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The roast pork component contributes saturated fat and advanced glycation end-products from high-temperature cooking, raising moderate cardiovascular concern. This dish is a staple lunch and dinner option in Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops, deeply embedded in Singaporean Chinese food culture.

confidence_reason: All core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010696) with a provenance confidence of 0.95. The dish is a canonical Singapore hawker item with an exact match in the primary regional approved source. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component (steamed white rice) using the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g (1.4 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for a comparable mixed roasted chicken and pork belly rice dish, as the HPB Foodo entry does not separately report saturated fat; this value should be updated when HPB publishes a disaggregated fatty acid profile. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient vitamins, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific composite hawker dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; this is confirmed zero per the HPB Foodo database entry — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo (G00010696). 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white rice (Asian-style) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish has been published. 5. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are mapped to broader ancestor classes as no exact codes exist for this specific Singaporean mixed-protein rice dish. 6. The dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork.
